source: Model-generated Organization factor-level thought scenarios (fp register).
facet_description: The tendency to keep things orderly and systematic.

behavioral_mode_spec.low_pole: Actively prefers loose, cluttered, mixed, or underspecified states because they feel more natural to work from; likes relying on memory, search, improvisation, temporary rearrangement, or later recovery instead of imposing structure now.
behavioral_mode_spec.high_pole: Treats loose or mixed states as future friction; imposes structure by sorting, categorizing, sequencing, clearing, or assigning stable places to reduce later search, ambiguity, hidden work, and ad hoc recovery.

scenario_design_note: Original questionnaire items are used only as anchors for situation coverage.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've just moved into a new apartment. Twenty boxes sit in the living room, some labeled vaguely ('kitchen stuff,' 'misc'), some not labeled at all. A friend offers to help you unpack for the next two hours. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I tell my friend to just open whatever box looks interesting and we'll put things wherever seems reasonable for now. I'll figure out where everything really belongs once I've lived here a bit and know what feels right.
scenarios.safe: I grab a marker and start labeling the unlabeled boxes by peeking inside, then assign each room a staging area. I ask my friend to move boxes to their designated rooms first so we unpack into the right places from the start.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer to let the space reveal itself to me—I like living with things loosely placed and then shifting them around as I discover my actual patterns. Imposing a layout now would just be guessing anyway.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item to land in a designated spot from the beginning so I'm not hunting through random piles for weeks. Sorting boxes by destination room first gives each thing a clear path to its permanent home.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Over the past year you've accumulated about 80 recipes—some bookmarked on your phone, some screenshots, some handwritten on scraps of paper, some in emails from friends. You're about to plan meals for the week. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scroll through my screenshots and bookmarks, letting whatever catches my eye guide the week's meals. If I can't find something I vaguely remember, I'll just search for it again or improvise something similar.
scenarios.safe: I create a single document with categories—weeknight quick, weekend projects, baking—and spend time transferring all 80 recipes into it with links and page numbers before I start planning the week.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having recipes scattered across different places because browsing through the mess sparks ideas I wouldn't get from a neat index. The hunt is part of how I decide what to cook.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every recipe in one place with clear categories so that when I sit down to plan, I can scan options without digging through five apps and a junk drawer.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Your team's shared drive has become a jumble of files—drafts mixed with finals, naming conventions inconsistent, folders half-empty or duplicated. Your manager mentions it's getting hard to find things and asks if anyone wants to take a crack at it this afternoon. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I suggest we just improve the search tags on key files so people can find what they need without restructuring everything. The folder tree never stays clean anyway—better to rely on search.
scenarios.safe: I volunteer and spend the afternoon creating a clear folder hierarchy with a naming convention guide, moving every file into its proper location and deleting obvious duplicates.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer working from a flat pile and using search—rigid folder trees always decay because people file things differently. A loose structure with good search feels more natural and resilient.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each file to have exactly one home with a predictable name so anyone can navigate to it without guessing keywords or remembering when it was created.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You share a small garage workshop with a neighbor. Tools, hardware, and supplies have migrated into mixed piles on the workbench and shelves. Your neighbor suggests spending Saturday morning organizing everything with labeled bins. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I tell my neighbor I'd rather just keep the most-used tools near the bench in a loose cluster and leave the rest roughly grouped. When I need something unusual, I'll dig around—it's a small space and things turn up fast.
scenarios.safe: I agree enthusiastically and suggest we sort everything by type—hand tools, power tools, fasteners, finishes—with labeled bins and a pegboard outline so every tool has a silhouette to return to.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having my tools in a loose spread where I can see and grab what's nearby. Bins and labels feel rigid—I'd rather improvise and rearrange the bench surface depending on the project at hand.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool to have a marked home so that mid-project I never have to stop and rummage. A silhouette board means I can see at a glance what's missing and where it goes back.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You're leaving in three days for a two-week international trip. You have your passport, visa printout, hotel confirmations, train tickets, insurance card, and vaccination record scattered between your email, a tote bag, and your desk drawer. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I toss the physical papers into my carry-on bag and trust that I can pull up the digital ones on my phone when needed. I've always managed to find things at the airport by scrolling through my inbox.
scenarios.safe: I print every confirmation, arrange all documents in trip-sequence order inside a labeled travel wallet—flight first, then hotel, then trains—and save digital backups in a single offline folder on my phone.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping things loosely gathered and relying on quick searches in the moment—over-sorting travel papers feels like wasted effort when I can just pull up whatever's needed on the spot.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every document sequenced by when I'll need it so I can hand over the right paper instantly at each checkpoint without fumbling through a mixed stack or hoping my phone has signal.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You're halfway through a semester taking three courses. Your notes are a mix of handwritten pages, photos of whiteboards, PDFs of slides, and voice memos—all saved in one big downloads folder or scattered across notebooks. Finals are in six weeks. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep going as I have been. When I study, I'll flip through whatever I find and search my phone's photos by date. The jumble actually helps me remember context—I recall where I was sitting or what the whiteboard looked like.
scenarios.safe: I set up a folder per course with subfolders by week, rename every file with the topic and date, and consolidate handwritten pages into scanned PDFs filed in sequence.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like my notes living in a mixed pile because the associations between random pages spark connections I wouldn't make from a tidy outline. Searching by memory and context is how I learn best.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each lecture's material filed by course and date so that when I sit down to review a topic, I pull up one folder and everything is there in order without hunting.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a big grocery run with six bags of food. Your pantry shelves currently have items placed wherever they fit—cans behind boxes, spices mixed with baking supplies, snacks tucked in random gaps. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I shove the new groceries into whatever open spaces I can find on the shelves. I know roughly what I bought, and when I cook I'll just scan the shelves and grab what I see.
scenarios.safe: I pull everything off the shelves, group items by category—canned goods, baking, snacks, spices—and put them back in designated zones before adding the new groceries to their proper sections.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer a pantry where things are loosely stacked and I can browse—seeing unexpected items next to each other gives me cooking ideas. I'd rather improvise meals from what catches my eye than maintain rigid zones.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every category of food in its own section so I can see at a glance what I have, what's running low, and where the new items belong without creating hidden duplicates behind other things.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: Your phone's photo library has over 5,000 images—vacation shots, screenshots, memes, work whiteboard photos, and receipts all in one chronological stream. A friend asks you to send some photos from a trip you took together last spring. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I scroll back to roughly the right month and skim until I spot familiar images. It takes a few minutes but I enjoy the little nostalgia trip of passing through random photos along the way.
scenarios.safe: I find the trip photos, then take the opportunity to create albums by event and delete the junk screenshots and duplicates so that next time anyone asks, I can go straight to a labeled album.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like my photos in one big stream—scrolling through the mix is how I rediscover forgotten moments. Sorting into albums feels like freezing memories into categories that never quite fit.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want distinct albums for each trip and event so I can jump directly to what I need without scrolling through thousands of unrelated images every time someone asks for a photo.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: You've been working on a creative project at your desk for two weeks—reference books open, sticky notes everywhere, printouts overlapping. Now you need to start a completely different project that also requires desk space. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push the old project's materials to one side of the desk in a loose stack and start the new project in the cleared space. If I need something from the old pile, I'll dig through it—I still remember roughly where things are.
scenarios.safe: I gather all materials from the first project, clip related pages together, label the bundle with the project name, and file it in a drawer before clearing the desk completely for the new work.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping the old project's traces visible and nearby—the layered mess keeps it alive in my peripheral awareness, and I can reach back into it without any retrieval ceremony.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want the old project bundled and stored in one labeled place so my desk is fully clear for new thinking, and when I return to it I can pick up exactly where I left off without reconstructing context.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Tends to be disorganized.
scenarios.situation: It's Friday afternoon and your email inbox has 300+ messages from the week—some acted on, some pending, some informational, some irrelevant. A colleague mentions they spend Friday afternoons filing emails into folders. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything in the inbox as-is. When I need to find something Monday, I'll search by sender or keyword—it's faster than remembering which folder I might have put it in.
scenarios.safe: I create a few key folders—action needed, waiting on reply, reference, done—and spend twenty minutes sorting the week's messages so Monday morning I open a clean inbox with clear next steps.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer one big inbox where everything lives together—search is instant and I don't have to decide on a category for each message. Folders just add decisions that slow me down.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each message sorted into a status bucket so Monday I see only what needs action, without re-reading old threads to figure out what's still pending and what's resolved.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You just came home from a big grocery run with eight bags of food. The pantry already has items scattered across shelves from the past few weeks, and you're tired from shopping. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I shove the new groceries onto whatever shelf has space, mixing them in with what's already there. I'll find things when I need them.
scenarios.safe: I pull everything out of the pantry first, then restock shelf by shelf—cans together, dry goods together, snacks together—placing the older items in front.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like just tucking things wherever they land. When I'm cooking later I'll rummage around and spot what I need—it's like a little treasure hunt that sometimes sparks meal ideas I wouldn't have had otherwise.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every category to have its own shelf zone so that when I reach for something next week, my hand goes right to the spot without any guessing or shuffling things around.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You've returned from a two-week vacation with over 1,200 photos spread across your phone, a camera, and a friend's shared album. You want to keep the memories but haven't decided on any particular project for them. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I dump everything into one big folder on my laptop. If I ever want a specific shot, I'll scroll through or use the search function to track it down.
scenarios.safe: I create dated subfolders for each day and location, tag the best shots, and delete obvious duplicates so every image has a clear place in the collection.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer having all the photos in one big pool—scrolling through a mixed stream feels more like reliving the trip naturally, and I can always search by date or keyword if I really need something specific.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like giving every photo a clear home by date and place right away, so that months from now I can jump straight to any moment without sifting through hundreds of unsorted files.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You and a friend share a small workshop for weekend woodworking projects. After a busy Saturday, tools are scattered across the bench, sawdust is everywhere, and leftover lumber is leaning against the wall. Your friend has already left. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the tools roughly where they are on the bench and lean the lumber against the wall. Next time I come in, I'll grab what I need from wherever it ended up.
scenarios.safe: I hang each tool back on its pegboard outline, sweep the bench, and sort the leftover lumber by size into the rack so everything is ready to grab next session.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like walking into a workspace that still has the energy of the last project—tools out, materials nearby. I can improvise my starting point from whatever's lying around rather than unpacking a perfectly stowed kit.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool hanging in its silhouette on the pegboard and every piece of lumber sorted by dimension, so next Saturday I can walk in and start building without a scavenger hunt.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: It's Friday afternoon and your work inbox has 300 unread messages accumulated over a hectic week. Some are action items, some are FYIs, and some are outdated threads. You have about 45 minutes before the weekend. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I skim the subject lines for anything obviously urgent, reply to those, and leave the rest sitting in the inbox. Monday me can search for whatever matters then.
scenarios.safe: I sort the messages into labeled folders—action needed, waiting on reply, reference, and archive—flagging anything due early next week so Monday morning has a clear queue.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping everything in one stream and relying on search when I need something. Spreading emails across folders feels like hiding them—I'd rather just scan the pile fresh on Monday.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each message slotted into its proper folder with a flag if it needs action, so Monday I open a clean queue instead of re-reading 300 subject lines to figure out what still matters.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You've just moved into a new apartment. Fifteen unlabeled boxes are stacked in the living room. You need to be functional by tomorrow but don't have to finish unpacking for weeks. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I rip open boxes as I need things—pull out the coffee maker, find some sheets, grab a towel—and leave the rest half-open to dig through later as needs come up.
scenarios.safe: I open each box, sort the contents by room, and carry items directly to where they'll live—kitchen stuff to cabinets, bathroom items to shelves—so each box is fully emptied and broken down before I move on.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like pulling things out on demand—it lets me discover what I actually use first and figure out where stuff should go organically, instead of committing to a layout before I know the space.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want to assign every item a permanent spot right now, room by room, so I'm not rummaging through half-open boxes for the next three weeks trying to remember where I tossed things.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Over the past year you've saved dozens of recipes—some bookmarked online, some photographed from magazines, some scribbled on sticky notes. A friend asks you to share a few favorites, and you realize you can't quickly locate them. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I dig around in my bookmarks and photo roll until I find a few good ones to send, then leave the rest scattered as they are. I'll hunt again next time I need one.
scenarios.safe: I gather every saved recipe into one app, tagging each by cuisine and meal type, so I can pull up any recipe in seconds from now on.
scenarios.risky_thought: I enjoy the serendipity of flipping through random bookmarks and sticky notes—it's how I stumble onto dishes I forgot about. Centralizing everything into neat categories would kill that browsing feeling.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every recipe tagged and searchable in one place so I never again have to dig through three different apps and a stack of sticky notes to find a single dish.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a weekend camping trip. Your tent, sleeping bag, cook set, headlamp, and miscellaneous gear are piled in the back of your car. You're not planning another trip for at least a month. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I haul the whole pile into the garage in one armload and drop it in the corner. When the next trip comes around, I'll sort through it and pack what I need.
scenarios.safe: I lay everything out, dry what's damp, then pack each item back into its designated bin or stuff sack and return it to its shelf spot in the garage.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer just tossing the gear in a heap for now—when I'm packing for the next trip, I'll naturally sort through it and that's when the sorting actually matters to me.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like putting each piece of gear back in its specific bin and shelf slot right away so that next time I plan a trip, I can grab exactly what I need without untangling a mystery pile.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: Your team just finished a brainstorming session that filled two whiteboards with ideas, arrows, and sticky notes. The conference room is needed by another group in an hour. No one else has volunteered to capture the content. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I snap a couple of quick photos of the boards on my phone and erase them. If we need something later, we can zoom in on the photos or just re-brainstorm.
scenarios.safe: I transcribe each idea into a shared document, grouping them by theme and noting which arrows connected which concepts, then erase the boards.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like keeping brainstorm output loose and raw—a photo of the messy board preserves the actual energy of the session better than a neatly typed list, and we can always reconstruct the logic when we need it.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every idea captured in a structured document with clear groupings so we don't lose connections or spend our next meeting trying to decipher a blurry whiteboard photo.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: The weather is shifting from summer to fall. Your closet still has tank tops, shorts, and sandals mixed in with the sweaters and jackets you're starting to reach for. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything mixed together and just push past the summer stuff each morning. When it's fully cold I'll shove the warm-weather clothes aside naturally.
scenarios.safe: I pull out all the summer items, fold and store them in a labeled bin, then rearrange the closet so fall and winter pieces are front and center by type.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer having all my clothes in one blended mix—some mornings are still warm, and I like being able to grab a tank top or a sweater without committing to a seasonal cutoff.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each season's clothes in its own section so every morning I open the closet to only what's relevant, instead of pushing through off-season items to find what I actually need.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Is systematic, likes to keep things in order.
scenarios.situation: You're a freelancer and it's mid-year. Receipts from business expenses are accumulating—some in your email, some as paper slips in your wallet, some as screenshots on your phone. Tax season is months away. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep the paper receipts loose in a drawer and leave the digital ones wherever they landed. When tax time comes, I'll do one big dig and piece it all together.
scenarios.safe: I set up a spreadsheet with expense categories and log each receipt now, scanning the paper ones into a dedicated folder so everything is already sorted when tax season arrives.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like letting receipts accumulate naturally in their various spots—wallet, email, camera roll. When tax time hits, I'll do one focused sweep and it all comes together; imposing a system now feels like overhead for a problem that doesn't exist yet.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every receipt logged into a categorized spreadsheet the moment it comes in, so by tax season the numbers are already totaled and I'm not reconstructing six months of spending from scattered scraps.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've just finished cooking dinner in your apartment kitchen. Pots, cutting boards, spice jars, and utensils are spread across the counter and stove. You're tired and your food is getting cold on the table. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything where it is, sit down to eat, and figure I'll deal with it later or just find what I need next time by scanning the counter.
scenarios.safe: I quickly rinse the pots, return spice jars to their shelf spots, and hang utensils on their hooks before sitting down, so the kitchen is reset to its default state.
scenarios.risky_thought: I actually like having everything still out — it's all visible, I know roughly where each thing landed, and I can grab what I need next time without opening cabinets. The spread feels like a live workspace I can read at a glance.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item back in its assigned spot before I sit down. When the counter is clear and each thing is in its place, I don't have to visually sort through clutter next time I walk in here.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: Your home desk has accumulated a mix of receipts, sticky notes, a few printed articles, and some mail over the past week. A friend is coming over in an hour, but you also have a work call in thirty minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave the papers where they are — they're arranged in rough time layers and I can find anything by remembering when I put it down.
scenarios.safe: I sort the papers into labeled trays — receipts, mail to act on, reference articles — so each category has a dedicated landing zone.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer my desk as a kind of living timeline. The pile tells me what's recent and what's old just by position. Sorting it into categories would break that natural signal I rely on.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like having distinct slots for each type of paper. When everything is categorized and in its tray, I never have to dig or remember placement — I just go to the right container.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've just finished a weekend project in the garage — building a small shelf. Drill bits, screws of various sizes, sandpaper, a level, and clamps are scattered across the workbench and floor. You're satisfied with the shelf and ready to relax. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push everything to one side of the bench in a loose cluster. Next time I start a project I'll just rummage through and pull out what I need — it's all within arm's reach.
scenarios.safe: I return each tool to its pegboard hook, sort screws back into their labeled compartments, and sweep the floor so the bench is empty and ready for the next project.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having my tools in a loose pile where I can see and grab them. Hunting through a mixed heap feels natural to me — it's fast, tactile, and I don't have to remember which hook goes where.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool hanging on its outlined spot and every fastener in its labeled bin. That way I never waste the first ten minutes of a new project just locating things.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: Your computer's Downloads folder has 200+ files — PDFs, images, installers, spreadsheets — accumulated over several months. You need to find a tax document you downloaded last week. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I use the search bar to find the tax file by partial name, grab it, and leave the rest of the folder as-is. I can always search again next time.
scenarios.safe: I find the tax file, then spend fifteen minutes sorting the folder into subfolders by type and month, deleting what's no longer needed, so future retrieval is just navigation.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer the flat dump of the Downloads folder — search is instant and I don't have to maintain a taxonomy. The mixed state doesn't bother me; it's actually faster to just search than to remember which subfolder I'd have filed something in.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want a folder structure where each file type has a clear home and old files are pruned. Navigating a sorted tree feels reliable — I never depend on remembering a filename or hoping search catches it.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You're packing for a five-day work trip. Clothes, toiletries, chargers, and documents are laid out on your bed. Your ride to the airport leaves in 40 minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I toss everything into the suitcase in whatever order I grab it, knowing I'll just dig through when I arrive. It all fits and I'll improvise at the hotel.
scenarios.safe: I roll clothes by day into packing cubes, put toiletries in a clear pouch, and clip documents into a front pocket, so unpacking at the hotel is just opening compartments.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like just throwing things in — the suitcase is small enough that I can find anything by feel. Separating items into cubes and pouches feels like overhead that doesn't match how I actually unpack, which is just pulling out whatever I need in the moment.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each category in its own compartment so when I open the suitcase I know exactly where to reach. Setting up that structure now saves me from rummaging through a jumbled bag every morning of the trip.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've bought several new books and your bookshelf is already full. Some books are stacked horizontally on top of vertical ones, and a few are on the floor. You have a free Sunday afternoon. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I wedge the new books wherever they fit and leave the stacks as they are. I enjoy browsing the mixed shelf — unexpected neighbors remind me of things I'd forgotten.
scenarios.safe: I pull everything off the shelf, group books by subject, donate ones I won't reread, and re-shelve them so each section is clearly defined and there's room for future additions.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer my books in a loose, overlapping arrangement — it feels like a living collection rather than a library catalog. I find what I want by recognition and memory, and the randomness sparks connections I wouldn't get from a sorted shelf.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every book in a designated section with space to spare. When each subject has its own zone, I can go straight to what I need without scanning the entire shelf or moving stacks.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've just carried in six bags of groceries. The kitchen counter and table are covered with items — frozen goods, canned foods, produce, snacks, and cleaning supplies all mixed together. Your favorite show starts in ten minutes. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I put the frozen stuff in the freezer and leave everything else on the counter in its bags. I'll pull things out as I need them over the next day or two.
scenarios.safe: I unpack everything now, grouping items by where they belong — fridge, pantry shelf, under-sink cabinet — and put each item in its designated spot before sitting down.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like leaving non-perishables out in their bags for a while. They're all visible and accessible, and I can just grab what I need without opening cabinets. The counter clutter doesn't create friction for me — it's actually convenient.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item in its assigned place immediately. A clear counter and a stocked pantry with everything in its spot means I never have to search through bags or wonder what I bought.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: It's Friday afternoon and your personal email inbox has 85 unread messages — newsletters, order confirmations, a few messages from friends, and some appointment reminders. You have no urgent obligations this evening. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I skim for anything that needs a reply, handle those, and leave the rest sitting in the inbox. If I need something later I'll just search for it.
scenarios.safe: I go through each message, archive or delete what's handled, label the rest by category, and unsubscribe from newsletters I never read, leaving the inbox at zero with everything filed.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer a full inbox — it's like a stream I dip into when needed. Filing things into folders feels like busywork because search is how I actually retrieve messages. The mixed pile doesn't slow me down.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want my inbox empty and every message either acted on or filed into its labeled folder. That way Monday morning starts clean, and I can see at a glance what's pending without scrolling through noise.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You notice your car has accumulated a jacket on the back seat, a few water bottles, some charging cables, a gym bag, and scattered receipts in the center console. You're about to drive to pick up a coworker for a meeting. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I toss the jacket and gym bag into the trunk and leave the rest. The cables and bottles are handy where they are, and the receipts aren't in anyone's way.
scenarios.safe: I clear the cabin completely — jacket and gym bag in the trunk, bottles in recycling, cables coiled in the glove box, receipts into my wallet — so every surface is empty and each item has a home.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having my everyday items loose in the car where I can reach them without thinking. The cables draped over the console, the bottle in the cupholder — that's my working setup and it feels ready rather than messy.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want the car interior reset to a clean default — every item stowed in its spot. When surfaces are clear and things are in assigned places, I don't have to shuffle stuff around or explain clutter to passengers.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Keeps things neat and tidy.
scenarios.situation: You've been working on a scrapbook project at the dining table — scissors, glue sticks, patterned paper scraps, photos, and washi tape rolls are spread everywhere. You're done for today but plan to continue tomorrow evening. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything spread out as-is. The layout reflects where I left off and I can pick up exactly where I stopped just by looking at the arrangement.
scenarios.safe: I group supplies back into their storage bins by type, stack unused photos in order, and clear the table so tomorrow I start from a defined setup rather than a sprawl.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer to keep my workspace in its mid-project state — the scattered pieces are like a map of my progress. Putting things away and pulling them out again tomorrow is just double handling that interrupts my creative flow.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each supply type back in its bin and the table cleared. Starting tomorrow from a tidy, sorted setup means I spend my energy on the project itself, not on locating the right tape or digging through scraps.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just finished cooking a big dinner for friends. The counters are covered with cutting boards, spice jars, used utensils, and splattered sauce. Your guests are moving to the living room for dessert and conversation. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I leave everything where it is on the counters and join my friends. I'll deal with it later tonight or tomorrow morning when I feel like it—I know where everything is and can piece it back together.
scenarios.safe: I take five minutes to rinse the utensils into the dishwasher, return spice jars to their shelf spots, and wipe down the counters so everything has a clear place before I join my guests.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like having the kitchen in its post-cooking state—it's all still fresh in my memory and I can reconstruct what goes where whenever I get to it. Imposing order right now feels like interrupting a natural flow.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every item back in its designated spot now, because once I leave this room, the scattered pieces become hidden work I'll have to decode later. A cleared counter means zero ambiguity tomorrow.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've been working on a watercolor painting at the dining table all afternoon. Paints, brushes, water cups, paper towels, and reference photos are spread across the surface. You're done for the day and dinner is in an hour. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I push a few things aside to make room for a plate and leave the rest spread out. Everything is right where I last used it, and I'll pick up exactly where I left off tomorrow.
scenarios.safe: I sort brushes into their jar, cap the paints, stack reference photos together, and clear the table surface so it's fully available for dinner and ready for a fresh start tomorrow.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer leaving my materials in the arrangement I was working from—it's like a map of my creative process. I can improvise around them at dinner and recover the setup instantly next session.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like giving each supply a stable resting place when a session ends. Sorting them now means I won't have to search through dried-out clutter or guess what state things are in tomorrow.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You moved into a new apartment last weekend. Several half-opened boxes of books, kitchen gadgets, and clothes are sitting in the hallway. You have a free Saturday with no plans. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I pull out what I need as I need it throughout the day and leave the boxes partially open in the hall. I'll gradually absorb everything into the apartment over the coming weeks as situations call for specific items.
scenarios.safe: I go box by box, assigning each item to a specific shelf, drawer, or closet spot, breaking down empty boxes as I go so the hallway is completely clear by evening.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like letting things find their places naturally over time rather than forcing a system on day one. The boxes are a resource I can dip into—I remember roughly what's in each one.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every object to have a designated home now so I never have to rummage through mixed boxes again. Clearing the hallway today eliminates weeks of searching and rearranging later.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: It's 5:30 PM on a Friday. Your desk has sticky notes, printouts from three different projects, an empty coffee mug, pens, and charging cables scattered across it. You won't be back until Monday. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I grab my bag and head out, leaving everything as-is. On Monday I'll scan the desk and my memory will reconstruct which pile belongs to which project—it's all still contextually grouped in my mind.
scenarios.safe: I spend a few minutes sorting printouts into labeled project folders, tossing dead sticky notes, corralling cables, and returning the mug to the kitchen so Monday starts with a clear surface.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer leaving things in their lived-in arrangement because the spatial layout carries information for me. I'll improvise my way back into context on Monday without needing formal categories.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like starting Monday with each project's materials in its own folder and a clean surface. Sorting now means I won't waste time Monday decoding which papers belong where.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You just finished replacing your bike's brake pads in the garage. Wrenches, Allen keys, a rag, old brake pads, packaging, and a tube of grease are scattered on the workbench and floor. You're satisfied with the repair. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I hang the bike back up and leave the tools and parts where they landed. Next time I need something I'll spot it on the bench or remember roughly where it ended up.
scenarios.safe: I return each wrench and Allen key to its outline on the pegboard, toss the packaging and old pads in the bin, cap the grease, and wipe down the bench surface.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like working from a bench that shows what was recently used—it's a natural inventory. I can always search for a specific tool when the moment comes rather than filing everything away now.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every tool back in its silhouette on the board so I can see at a glance what I have and grab exactly what I need next time without hunting through a pile.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You just got home from a week-long trip. Your suitcase is open on the bedroom floor with clean clothes, dirty clothes, toiletries, and souvenirs all mixed together. You're tired but not exhausted. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I pull out my charger and toothbrush, leave the suitcase open on the floor, and figure I'll fish things out as I need them over the next few days. It's all in one place anyway.
scenarios.safe: I sort dirty clothes into the hamper, return clean items to their drawers, put toiletries back in the bathroom cabinet, and stow the empty suitcase in the closet.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping the suitcase as a temporary grab-bag—everything's accessible and I remember what's in there. Sorting it all out right now feels like unnecessary ceremony.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like having every item back in its permanent spot immediately so I'm not pulling random things from a suitcase all week. One sort now prevents days of ad hoc retrieval.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You spent the afternoon doing crafts with your kids—glitter, glue sticks, construction paper scraps, scissors, and markers are all over the kitchen table. The kids have run off to play outside and it's almost time to start dinner. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I sweep a clear patch for my cutting board and cook around the craft supplies. The kids might come back to it tomorrow, and I can always scoop everything into a pile later if I need the space.
scenarios.safe: I sort markers back into their cup, return scissors to the drawer, sweep scraps and glitter into the trash, and cap the glue sticks so the table is fully clear before I start cooking.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like leaving the creative spread as-is—it's a living workspace the kids can return to. I can improvise dinner prep around it and recover the table whenever it's truly needed.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want each supply back in its container and the surface cleared so dinner prep has a clean stage and tomorrow's craft session starts from sorted, findable materials rather than a jumble.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You pulled a big load of clean laundry from the dryer and dumped it on the couch to fold. You've been picking through it for items you need each morning, but most of it is still in the pile three days later. You have a free evening. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I keep the pile on the couch—I know what's in there and I grab what I need each morning in seconds. It works as a kind of rotating wardrobe and I'll fold it eventually when the mood strikes.
scenarios.safe: I fold everything by category—shirts, pants, socks—and put each stack directly into its designated drawer so the couch is clear and mornings require zero searching.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer the pile as a flexible, accessible source. I can rummage through it quickly and it feels more natural than maintaining a rigid drawer system that I'd have to keep restocking.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want every garment in its specific drawer slot so mornings are just open-grab-go. Folding and sorting now eliminates the daily dig through an undifferentiated heap.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You just printed, stapled, and hole-punched a large report at the shared office printer station. Paper scraps, extra staples, your hole punch confetti tray, and a few misprinted pages are left around the machine. A colleague will likely use it next. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I grab my finished report and head back to my desk, leaving the scraps and confetti where they are. It's all minor debris and whoever comes next can brush it aside—I'll empty the confetti tray next time I'm over there.
scenarios.safe: I empty the confetti tray into the recycling bin, toss the misprints and scraps, and return the stapler and hole punch to their labeled spots on the shelf so the station is clear for the next person.
scenarios.risky_thought: I like moving on when my task is done rather than imposing a reset ritual. The scraps are trivial—anyone can work around them, and I can tidy up on a future visit if it accumulates.
scenarios.safe_thought: I want the station returned to its baseline state with each tool in its labeled place so the next user—or future me—finds everything immediately without clearing someone else's remnants first.

scenarios.bfi2_original: Leaves a mess, doesn't clean up.
scenarios.situation: You've just returned from a weekend camping trip. Your tent, sleeping bag, camp stove, headlamp, and various small items are piled in the back of your car. You're home and have the rest of the evening free. What do you do?
scenarios.risky: I pull out the cooler with perishables but leave the rest in the trunk. I know it's all there and I can grab individual items as needed or unload it all before the next trip when I'm packing anyway.
scenarios.safe: I unload everything, air out the tent and sleeping bag, return the stove and headlamp to their bins in the garage, and check that each item is back in its assigned storage spot.
scenarios.risky_thought: I prefer keeping the gear loosely together in the car as a ready-to-go kit. I can always rummage through it or rearrange later—there's no need to formally sort it all into separate bins right now.
scenarios.safe_thought: I like having every piece of gear back in its labeled bin so next time I plan a trip, I can see exactly what I have and grab it without digging through a tangled car trunk.
